questions.question: Is the pre-approval inspection (PAI) strategy defined with appropriate facility readiness and timing?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a PAI strategy is documented, including identification of all facilities requiring inspection (DS, DP, testing, packaging), inspection readiness assessment for each site, anticipated inspection timeline relative to BLA submission, and remediation plans for known facility gaps. Assessment of whether mock inspections have been conducted.
questions.rationale: Pre-approval inspection is mandatory for BLAs (unlike NDAs where PAI may be risk-based). Facility inspection failure is the single largest cause of BLA CRLs — over 50% of CMC-related CRLs cite facility inspection deficiencies. PAI readiness must be a programme-level regulatory priority, not a last-minute CMC activity.

questions.question: Is the immunogenicity regulatory strategy defined, including labeling approach and ADA data presentation?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the regulatory approach to immunogenicity is documented: ADA incidence reporting format, neutralising antibody data presentation, clinical impact analysis (PK, efficacy, safety), integration into USPI Section 6.2, and alignment with FDA/EMA immunogenicity guidance. Assessment of whether the immunogenicity narrative is consistent across IB, Clinical Overview (Module 2.5), and proposed labeling.
questions.rationale: Immunogenicity reporting is a regulatory requirement for all biologics with no SM equivalent. The ADA data presentation, statistical approach to incidence reporting, and clinical impact interpretation must be prospectively planned and consistently presented across regulatory documents. Poor immunogenicity data presentation is a common source of FDA review questions.

questions.question: Is the comparability strategy for process changes regulatorily aligned and prospectively documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a regulatory comparability strategy is in place that defines: which process changes require comparability assessment, the analytical, functional, and (if needed) clinical approach for each level of change, prospective comparability protocols agreed with agencies, and filing strategy (supplement type) for post-approval changes. Assessment of whether the strategy is aligned with ICH Q5E and FDA's comparability guidance.
questions.rationale: Process changes are inevitable in biologic development (scale-up, site transfer, optimisation). Each change requires regulatory assessment and potentially filing. A prospective comparability strategy — ideally with FDA-agreed comparability protocols — can save years of post-approval change implementation time. Programmes without this strategy face repeated supplement filings and potential clinical bridging requirements.

questions.question: Is the BPCIA exclusivity and biosimilar competitive landscape assessed and integrated into lifecycle strategy?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the 12-year BPCIA reference product exclusivity timeline is mapped, biosimilar competitive landscape is monitored (including 351(k) applications, patent dance proceedings, interchangeability designations), and lifecycle strategy (indication expansions, formulation improvements, device innovation) is designed to extend competitive position beyond exclusivity expiry.
questions.rationale: BPCIA provides 12-year reference product exclusivity for biologics (vs. 5-year NCE for SM under Hatch-Waxman), creating a different competitive landscape. Biosimilar competition dynamics, patent dance procedures, and interchangeability designations are unique to biologics. Strategic lifecycle planning to defend against biosimilar entry is a critical regulatory-commercial capability.

questions.question: Is the BIMO (Bioresearch Monitoring) inspection readiness strategy defined alongside the manufacturing PAI, including clinical site selection for inspection, data integrity verification, BDRG (BIMO Data Reviewer's Guide) preparation, and standardized electronic submission format per FDA December 2024 guidance?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether BLA inspection readiness covers both manufacturing PAI and clinical BIMO: BIMO Data Reviewer's Guide preparation per FDA standardized format guidance (Dec 2024); clinical site identification for likely BIMO inspection (highest-enrolling sites, sites with data quality signals); site-level data integrity verification and pre-inspection audit; manufacturing PAI readiness for biologic-specific areas (aseptic processing, cell banking, cold chain, analytical methods); PAI scheduling strategy relative to PDUFA date; and contingency planning for PAI findings (re-inspection timeline, response strategy).
questions.rationale: FDA's published CRLs revealed that facility inspection deficiencies were cited in the majority of CMC-related BLA CRLs — 25 of 43 in July 2025 and all 27 in September 2025. For biologics, PAI is especially complex (aseptic manufacturing, cell bank characterisation, viral clearance validation). BIMO inspection of clinical sites can reveal data integrity issues that derail approval. The BDRG is now expected in standardised format. Both PAI and BIMO must be planned as critical-path BLA activities, not afterthoughts.

questions.question: Is the BPCIA patent and exclusivity strategy defined, including Purple Book listing, patent dance preparation, 12-year BLA exclusivity timeline, and proactive lifecycle management against future biosimilar competition?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether BPCIA strategy includes: understanding of 12-year BLA exclusivity period and 4-year data exclusivity provisions; Purple Book listing strategy (product, strength, dosage form, route, BLA number, patent information); patent portfolio assessment relevant to BPCIA litigation (composition, formulation, method of use, manufacturing process patents); patent dance readiness (180-day exchange of patent information, 60-day response); proactive lifecycle management (new indications, new formulations, new devices) to extend commercial protection; and monitoring of biosimilar development programs targeting the reference product.
questions.rationale: BPCIA provides 12 years of BLA exclusivity and a complex patent dispute resolution process (the "patent dance") unique to biologics. Unlike the Hatch-Waxman framework for small molecules, BPCIA exclusivity and patent litigation have distinct timelines and procedures. Sponsors must plan Purple Book listing, patent portfolio development, and lifecycle management strategies from early development — not post-approval. Several biologic products have faced biosimilar competition within years of approval, demonstrating the importance of proactive BPCIA strategy.

questions.question: Are version control and change management procedures documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether change management processes are in place.
questions.rationale: Robust change control is essential for submission consistency and inspection readiness.

questions.question: Are regulatory-impact assessments conducted for CMC or clinical changes?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether regulatory impact is assessed for changes. For biologics, includes assessment of whether CMC process changes trigger comparability requirements (ICH Q5E), whether regulatory filing category (PAS, CBE-30, Annual Report) is determined for each change, and whether comparability protocols are prospectively agreed with agencies.
questions.rationale: For biologics, every manufacturing process change has regulatory filing implications — the filing category (Prior Approval Supplement vs. CBE-30 vs. Annual Report) depends on the change's risk to product quality. Prospective comparability protocols (agreed with FDA) can streamline post-approval changes. This is a critical biologic-specific regulatory capability.

questions.question: Are variations, amendments, and renewals tracked and submitted on time?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether submissions are current.
questions.rationale: Timely submission of variations prevents compliance gaps.

questions.question: Is the regulatory strategy for manufacturing site transfers (clinical-scale CDMO to commercial-scale facility) defined, including comparability data requirements, supplement filing timing relative to BLA, and contingency planning for incomplete site transfer at time of submission?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether site transfer regulatory planning includes: comparability protocol for site transfer (analytical, functional, process comparability); regulatory filing strategy (include commercial site in original BLA vs post-approval supplement); PAI scheduling for both clinical and commercial manufacturing sites; timing of site transfer completion relative to BLA submission and PDUFA date; contingency planning if site transfer is incomplete at BLA filing (impact on approval, post-marketing commitments); and global harmonisation of site transfer filings across FDA/EMA/PMDA.
questions.rationale: Biologic manufacturing site transfers are a major regulatory risk that does not exist for small molecules (where manufacturing is simpler and site transfers are routine). Transferring a biologic manufacturing process from clinical-scale CDMO to commercial-scale facility requires extensive comparability data. If the commercial site is not included in the original BLA, a post-approval supplement is required before commercial launch from the new site — potentially delaying launch by 6-12 months. This timing must be strategically planned.

questions.question: Is the combination product regulatory pathway defined for drug-device presentations (PFS, autoinjector, on-body injector)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the regulatory pathway for combination products is determined (FDA: primary mode of action determination, lead CDER/CBER center, 21 CFR Part 4 compliance; EU: MDR/IVDR interface with pharmaceutical legislation). Assessment of whether the combination product designation request has been filed with FDA OCP.
questions.rationale: Most biologic products are administered via prefilled syringe, autoinjector, or on-body injector — making them combination products under FDA regulation. The regulatory pathway (CDER vs. CBER primary jurisdiction, combination product agreement) must be established early. Device-related requirements (human factors, design verification/validation, cGMP for devices) add significant regulatory complexity.

questions.question: Are human factors engineering studies planned or completed for self-administration devices?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether human factors/usability studies (formative and summative) are planned per FDA guidance. Assessment of whether the target user population is defined, use-related risk analysis is conducted, and the study programme supports the combination product submission.
questions.rationale: FDA requires human factors engineering studies for self-administration devices (autoinjectors, PFS for self-injection, on-body injectors). These studies — particularly summative/validation human factors studies — are BLA submission requirements that are frequently underestimated in timeline and cost. Human factors study failures can delay BLA submission.

questions.question: Is device design control documentation (Design History File) integrated with the BLA?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a Design History File (DHF) per 21 CFR 820 (now harmonised with ISO 13485 under QMSR) is maintained for the device constituent, and whether device documentation is integrated into the BLA submission (typically in Module 3.2.P.7 or a separate device section).
questions.rationale: Combination product cGMP requires compliance with both drug and device quality system requirements. The Design History File documents the device development lifecycle and is inspectable by FDA. Integration of device documentation into the BLA requires cross-functional coordination between drug and device regulatory teams.

questions.question: Are registration requirements evaluated for China, LATAM, MENA, SEA, Africa?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether emerging market requirements are evaluated. For biologics, includes assessment of country-specific biologic classification, biosimilar pathways, and local clinical data requirements.
questions.rationale: Emerging market biologic registration can differ significantly from SM registration — some regions classify mAbs differently, have distinct biosimilar frameworks, or require additional local clinical data.

questions.question: Are local regulatory agents or affiliates engaged?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether local partners are in place.
questions.rationale: Local partners are essential for navigating complex emerging market requirements.

questions.question: Are local-language dossiers and administrative documents planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether local documentation is planned.
questions.rationale: Local-language documentation is a top operational cause of emerging market delays.

questions.question: Has an IND (FDA) or CTA (EU, other regions) been submitted and accepted?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether IND/CTA is submitted. For biologics, IND must include biologic-specific content: cell line characterisation, viral safety data, immunogenicity risk assessment, and for bispecifics, MABEL-based dose justification.
questions.rationale: Biologic INDs require additional content beyond SM INDs — cell substrate information, viral safety assessment, and immunogenicity risk assessment. These additional requirements increase preparation time and complexity.

questions.question: Are all relevant modules (CMC, nonclinical, clinical) included and complete?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether CTD modules are complete. For biologics, Module 3 (Quality) is substantially more complex (cell line, upstream/downstream process, viral safety, comparability). Module 4 (Nonclinical) must include tissue cross-reactivity studies. Module 2 summaries must address biologic-specific topics.
questions.rationale: BLA Module 3 is the most complex section — and the most frequent source of CRLs. For biologics, Module 3 requires cell substrate characterisation, viral safety data, reference standard information, and comparability data that have no SM parallel.

questions.question: Are country-specific requirements addressed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether local requirements are met.
questions.rationale: Country-specific requirements must be addressed to avoid trial delays.

questions.question: Have any clinical holds, deficiencies, or questions been received and resolved?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether holds are resolved. For biologics, includes assessment of whether CMC-related clinical holds (common for biologics due to manufacturing complexity) are resolved with adequate comparability data and facility remediation.
questions.rationale: Biologic programmes are disproportionately subject to CMC-related clinical holds — manufacturing complexity, viral safety gaps, and insufficient characterisation data are common triggers.

questions.question: Is Module 1 complete for at least one region?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether Module 1 is compiled.
questions.rationale: Module 1 completeness is the gatekeeper to submission acceptance.

questions.question: Are overviews (Quality, Nonclinical, Clinical) well-written, integrated, and up to date?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether Module 2 overviews are sound. For biologics, the Quality Overall Summary (2.3) must address cell line, viral safety, comparability, and biologic-specific specifications — substantially more complex than SM Quality Overview.
questions.rationale: The BLA Quality Overall Summary (Module 2.3) is a critical document that must synthesise complex biologic CMC information (cell line history, process development rationale, viral safety, comparability) into a coherent regulatory narrative. Poor quality of this document is a common source of FDA review questions.

questions.question: Are inconsistencies across modules addressed and reconciled?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether cross-module consistency is maintained.
questions.rationale: Inconsistencies are among the most common causes of major objections.

questions.question: Has orphan drug designation been granted or filed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether ODD is pursued across jurisdictions.
questions.rationale: ODD provides critical regulatory and commercial incentives including market exclusivity.

questions.question: Are rare disease justifications supported by epidemiologic data?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether prevalence data supports ODD.
questions.rationale: Epidemiologic justification is the cornerstone of ODD eligibility.

questions.question: Are Paediatric Study Plans submitted or waivers obtained?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PSP/PIP compliance is in place. For biologics, includes biologic-specific paediatric considerations (FcRn maturation, immune system development, age-appropriate formulation/device).
questions.rationale: PSP/PIP compliance is critical. For biologics, paediatric plans must address biologic-specific issues including age-dependent FcRn maturation affecting PK and immune system development affecting PD.

questions.question: Has the asset been evaluated for RMAT, BTD, PRIME, or other expedited designations?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether expedited designations are evaluated. For biologics, RMAT is relevant for cell/gene therapies; BTD is commonly pursued for bispecifics and novel-target mAbs in oncology.
questions.rationale: Bispecifics and novel mAbs have high BTD grant rates in oncology. Strategic pursuit provides intensive FDA guidance and rolling review.

questions.question: Are Risk Management Plans (RMPs), REMS, or pharmacovigilance plans under development?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether risk management is in development. For biologics, includes immunogenicity monitoring in post-marketing, CRS management protocols in REMS (for bispecifics), and enhanced pharmacovigilance for novel mechanisms.
questions.rationale: Biologic REMS may include CRS mitigation strategies (step-up dosing requirements, prescriber certification, healthcare facility requirements) that are unique to immune-engaging biologics.

questions.question: Are pharmacovigilance systems compliant with EU/US requirements?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether PV systems are compliant.
questions.rationale: PV non-compliance is a leading cause of critical regulatory findings.

questions.question: Are post-approval commitments anticipated and resourced?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether commitments are planned. For biologics, includes post-marketing immunogenicity monitoring, confirmatory studies for accelerated approvals (common for bispecifics), and post-marketing comparability studies if process changes are planned.
questions.rationale: Biologics frequently receive accelerated approval requiring confirmatory studies. Post-approval immunogenicity monitoring is often a specific commitment. Process changes post-approval require comparability assessment and regulatory filing.

questions.question: Are label extensions, post-marketing studies, or conditional approvals considered?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether lifecycle strategies are planned.
questions.rationale: Proactive lifecycle planning is essential for commercial success and sustained market access.

questions.question: Is the post-approval change management framework for biologics understood and planned for, including the distinction between Prior Approval Supplements (PAS), Changes Being Effected in 30 days (CBE-30), and annual report changes, with biologic-specific change categorisation criteria?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether post-approval regulatory planning includes: understanding of FDA change categorisation for biologics (PAS required for changes that have substantial potential to affect safety/efficacy; CBE-30 for moderate changes; annual report for minor changes); biologic-specific change examples (cell bank changes typically PAS; process parameter changes may be CBE-30 or PAS depending on impact; analytical method changes may be annual report or CBE-30); comparability requirements for each change category; global regulatory harmonisation of post-approval changes (EMA Type II/Type IB/Type IA variations vs FDA PAS/CBE-30); and proactive lifecycle change planning (anticipating needed changes and pre-planning regulatory pathway).
questions.rationale: Post-approval change management for biologics is fundamentally more complex than for small molecules. Manufacturing process changes that would be annual reportable for a small molecule tablet may require a Prior Approval Supplement for a biologic because of the potential impact on product quality attributes. Understanding this framework before approval — and planning anticipated changes against the correct regulatory pathway — prevents post-approval delays and compliance issues. Many sponsors underestimate the regulatory burden of post-approval biologic manufacturing changes.

questions.question: Is the eCTD backbone structure compliant and validated?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether eCTD structure is compliant.
questions.rationale: Technical non-compliance is a top reason for validation failure.

questions.question: Are hyperlinks functional and folders correctly labelled?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether navigation is functional.
questions.rationale: Broken hyperlinks cause validation failures and damage reviewer confidence.

questions.question: Are previous agency queries tracked and responded to in updated modules?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether queries are tracked and resolved.
questions.rationale: Systematic query tracking prevents repeated deficiencies.

questions.question: Is an experienced publisher or regulatory operations partner in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether experienced RegOps capability is assigned.
questions.rationale: Experienced publishing and RegOps capability is essential for submission quality.

questions.question: Has a plan been defined for BLA/MAA submission timing and format?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the submission plan is documented. For biologics, includes BLA (not NDA) pathway planning, rolling submission strategy (if BTD granted), and pre-BLA meeting timing relative to facility inspection scheduling.
questions.rationale: BLA submissions have additional requirements beyond NDA — mandatory pre-approval inspection, immunogenicity section, biologic-specific Module 3. Rolling submission (available with Breakthrough Therapy Designation) can significantly accelerate BLA review.

questions.question: Are region-specific approval pathways chosen?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether regional pathways are selected and documented.
questions.rationale: Strategic pathway selection can accelerate approval and enable faster patient access.

questions.question: Are bridging strategies and waivers identified for global submissions?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether bridging and waiver strategies are defined. For biologics, includes comparability-based bridging for process/site changes (ICH Q5E), ethnic sensitivity assessment for biologics (which differs from SM), and waivers for organ impairment studies (mAbs not renally/hepatically cleared).
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific bridging includes CMC comparability bridging (ICH Q5E), clinical PK comparability for process changes, and scientific justification for organ impairment study waivers. These biologic-specific bridging strategies must be prospectively planned and agreed with agencies.

questions.question: Are registration-enabling studies complete or scheduled?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether registration-enabling studies are tracked.
questions.rationale: Completeness of registration-enabling studies is the primary gatekeeper for submission readiness.

questions.question: For programs pursuing accelerated approval, is the confirmatory trial regulatory strategy prospectively defined, including trial design agreement with FDA (SPA if applicable), enrollment diligence monitoring plan, withdrawal risk assessment, and conversion-to-traditional-approval timeline?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether accelerated approval regulatory strategy includes: confirmatory trial design discussed and agreed with FDA before accelerated approval filing; Special Protocol Assessment (SPA) for confirmatory trial if applicable; post-accelerated-approval enrollment diligence monitoring and reporting plan (FDA increasingly scrutinises enrollment pace); withdrawal risk assessment if confirmatory trial fails or is delayed; ODAC review contingency planning for confirmatory data; conversion-to-traditional-approval submission timeline; and commercial planning aligned with regulatory uncertainty during confirmatory period.
questions.rationale: Accelerated approval for biologics carries unique regulatory risks. Belantamab mafodotin was voluntarily withdrawn in 2023 after confirmatory trial failure. FDA held a dedicated ODAC in 2025 reviewing accelerated approval products. The confirmatory trial strategy must be prospectively defined — trial design, SPA, enrollment diligence, and withdrawal contingency. FDA is increasingly willing to withdraw accelerated approvals when confirmatory evidence is inadequate. This is a program-defining regulatory risk that requires explicit strategy.

questions.question: Has a rolling submission or real-time review strategy been evaluated for the BLA, with module-by-module submission timing optimised relative to CMC readiness, clinical data maturity, and PDUFA date targeting?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether filing strategy includes: assessment of rolling submission eligibility (Fast Track, Breakthrough designations enable rolling review); module-by-module submission sequence optimisation (Module 3/CMC often ready before Module 5/Clinical for biologics); pre-submission meeting with FDA to discuss rolling review logistics; PDUFA date targeting based on last module submission; risk assessment of partial submission (FDA questions on already-submitted modules while later modules pending); and coordination between rolling BLA submission and PAI/BIMO inspection scheduling.
questions.rationale: Rolling submission allows sponsors to submit BLA modules as they are completed rather than waiting for the entire package. For biologics where CMC data (Module 3) may be ready months before final clinical data (Module 5), rolling submission can significantly accelerate time to PDUFA. However, it requires careful planning — FDA may raise questions on early modules before later modules are submitted, and PAI scheduling must align with module submission. This strategic decision is particularly relevant for Breakthrough and Fast Track designated biologics.

questions.question: Are all submission documents finalised, QA-checked, and archived?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether documentation is submission-ready.
questions.rationale: Documentation readiness is critical for inspection and submission.

questions.question: Are submissions inspection-ready (e.g., QA certificates, GLP/GCP statements)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether inspection readiness is demonstrated.
questions.rationale: Pre-approval inspection is mandatory for BLAs. Inspection readiness must be demonstrated before submission.

questions.question: Are Biologics Master Files or cross-references to CDMO filings appropriately managed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether regulatory filings for contract manufacturers, cell banks, or platform technologies are appropriately cross-referenced in the BLA. Assessment of whether CDMO Type II DMF or Biologics Master File cross-references are in place and current.
questions.rationale: Unlike SM (which commonly use Drug Master Files/ASMFs), biologic programmes typically include full CMC disclosure in the BLA. However, CDMOs may hold proprietary platform information in Type II DMFs that must be cross-referenced. Managing these cross-references and ensuring CDMO filing currency is a critical regulatory coordination task.

questions.question: Are correspondence logs and submission trackers complete and maintained?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether tracking systems are current.
questions.rationale: Complete tracking is essential for governance and inspection.

questions.question: Have pre-IND, IND, pre-BLA, or scientific advice meetings occurred?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether critical agency interactions have occurred. For biologics, includes pre-BLA meetings (mandatory discussion of CMC comparability, facility inspection readiness), Type A/B/C meetings covering biologic-specific topics (immunogenicity strategy, dose optimisation, combination product regulatory pathway), and for bispecifics, FIH dose selection discussions.
questions.rationale: Pre-BLA meetings for biologics are particularly critical because they address CMC comparability strategy, facility inspection scheduling, and immunogenicity data presentation — topics that can make or break first-cycle BLA approval.

questions.question: Are meeting minutes available and are regulatory feedback points addressed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether feedback is documented and actioned.
questions.rationale: Systematic feedback management ensures programme alignment with agency expectations.

questions.question: Are written agency communications documented, archived, and tracked for actions?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether communications are documented and tracked.
questions.rationale: Documented regulatory communications are critical for inspection readiness and submission consistency.

questions.question: Have global interactions (e.g., EMA SAWP, PMDA consultation) been planned or executed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether global interactions are planned. For biologics, includes engagement with agencies on biologic-specific topics (EMA SAWP on comparability, PMDA consultation on viral safety, FDA on immunogenicity strategy).
questions.rationale: Global agency interactions for biologics often involve biologic-specific topics that require specialised regulatory expertise — comparability strategy, viral safety, immunogenicity — that differ from typical SM advice topics.

questions.question: Is an Advisory Committee (AdComm) preparation strategy developed, including likelihood assessment, briefing document authoring plan, voting question strategy, panel composition review, dry-run rehearsal schedule, and contingency planning for negative vote outcomes?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether AdComm preparation includes: assessment of likelihood that FDA will convene an AdComm for this biologic program (novel mechanism, first-in-class, safety concerns, CRS risk, immunogenicity questions increase likelihood); briefing document authoring timeline and cross-functional review process; voting question strategy development in collaboration with FDA; panel composition review for relevant expertise and potential conflicts; dry-run rehearsal with mock panel and adversarial questioning; backup data packages for anticipated AdComm questions; media and investor communication strategy for all vote outcomes; and post-AdComm response strategy if FDA goes against panel recommendation.
questions.rationale: Advisory Committee meetings are among the highest-stakes regulatory events in biologic development. FDA convened AdComm for multiple biologic programs in 2024-2025, and went against AdComm recommendations 43% of the time in 2025 — an unprecedented rate. For biologics with novel mechanisms, CRS risk, or immunogenicity concerns, AdComm is increasingly likely. Preparation requires months of cross-functional effort. Sponsors who are unprepared for AdComm risk public exposure of weaknesses in their data package.

questions.question: Are relevant precedents (approvals in the same indication, target, or biologic format) identified and analysed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether precedent analysis covers approved biologics with the same target, indication, or format. For bispecifics, includes analysis of approved bispecific antibody regulatory packages (14+ FDA-approved bispecifics as of 2024), dose optimisation approaches, CRS management strategies in approved labels, and step-up dosing precedents.
questions.rationale: The bispecific antibody class is rapidly maturing with substantial regulatory precedent now available. Analysis of approved bispecific labels, FDA review documents, and ODAC discussions provides critical intelligence for dose selection, CRS management, and labeling strategy. Failing to leverage this precedent is a missed opportunity.

questions.question: Have comparator products' regulatory histories been reviewed (e.g., label restrictions, REMS, biosimilar landscape)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether comparator regulatory histories are analysed. For biologics, includes review of biosimilar competition timelines (BPCIA exclusivity expiry), patent dance outcomes, interchangeability designations, and whether comparator biologics have faced post-marketing dose modifications or safety label changes.
questions.rationale: Biosimilar competition dynamics are fundamentally different from generic competition — BPCIA provides 12-year exclusivity (vs. 5-year NCE for SM), but the patent dance and interchangeability pathway create unique competitive risks. Understanding the biosimilar landscape for competing biologics informs lifecycle strategy.

questions.question: Are guidance documents (ICH, FDA, EMA) fully integrated into development plans?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether biologic-specific guidance is integrated. For biologics, the relevant ICH guidances include Q5A-E (viral safety, cell substrates, stability, comparability, specifications), S6(R1) (nonclinical safety), Q6B (biologic specifications), and modality-specific FDA/EMA guidances (bispecific antibody guidance, immunogenicity guidance, biosimilar guidance). Assessment of whether Project Optimus dose optimisation guidance is integrated for oncology biologics.
questions.rationale: The biologic regulatory guidance landscape is distinct from SM — the ICH Q5 series, ICH S6(R1), and FDA immunogenicity and bispecific guidances are foundational. Non-integration of these biologic-specific guidances is a primary cause of regulatory findings and CRLs.

questions.question: Has the team systematically reviewed FDA's published CRL database (291 CRLs, July-September 2025) to identify BLA-specific deficiency patterns and proactively de-risk their own submission against common CRL causes (CMC/facility deficiencies, efficacy evidence gaps, immunogenicity data adequacy, labeling issues)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether CRL intelligence has been integrated: systematic review of BLA-specific CRLs from the published database; identification of deficiency patterns relevant to the biologic program (CMC/facility inspection failures as #1 cause; efficacy evidence gaps; immunogenicity data presentation; labeling deficiencies); proactive assessment of own submission against identified CRL patterns; gap analysis and mitigation plan for areas where the program shows CRL-risk characteristics; and documented lessons-learned integrated into the regulatory strategy.
questions.rationale: FDA published 291 CRLs in July and September 2025, creating an unprecedented public database of BLA/NDA rejection reasons. Analysis reveals that 74% of CRLs cite CMC deficiencies, facility inspection failures are the most common BLA CRL cause, and sponsors historically under-report 85% of FDA's concerns. This database is a free masterclass in what causes BLA failure. Any regulatory team that has not systematically reviewed relevant CRLs to de-risk their own filing is leaving easily accessible intelligence on the table.

questions.question: Is there a formal, written global regulatory strategy document?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a formal global regulatory strategy exists, is current, and covers BLA/MAA pathway selection, biosimilar landscape, biologic-specific designation strategy, immunogenicity regulatory plan, and comparability strategy for process changes. For biologics, includes BLA (351(a)) vs biosimilar (351(k)) positioning and BPCIA exclusivity planning.
questions.rationale: A formal global regulatory strategy is foundational. For biologics, strategy must address BLA-specific requirements (mandatory pre-approval inspection, comparability data for process changes, immunogenicity reporting), BPCIA 12-year exclusivity timing, and biosimilar competitive landscape — considerations that do not exist for NDA-pathway small molecules.

questions.question: Are country-specific pathways (e.g., FDA, EMA, PMDA, NMPA, TGA) addressed?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether country-specific pathways are addressed. For biologics, includes assessment of regional differences in biologic classification (biologic vs. drug), biosimilar pathways (US 351(k), EU biosimilar, WHO prequalification), and regional requirements for immunogenicity data, viral safety, and cell substrate information.
questions.rationale: Regional biologic regulatory requirements vary more significantly than for small molecules — the definition of "biologic" differs across jurisdictions, biosimilar pathways are not harmonised, and some regions have additional requirements for viral safety documentation or local clinical data.

questions.question: Are regulatory plans synchronised with clinical, CMC, and commercial timelines?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether regulatory plans align with development milestones. For biologics, includes specific attention to CMC-regulatory interdependency (process changes triggering comparability filings, pre-approval inspection timing, facility registration requirements) and the typically longer biologic development timelines.
questions.rationale: For biologics, CMC-regulatory synchronisation is more critical than for SM because process changes require regulatory comparability assessments and potentially prior approval supplements. Pre-approval inspection is mandatory for BLAs, adding a fixed lead-time requirement. 74% of BLA Complete Response Letters cite CMC deficiencies — regulatory oversight of CMC readiness is paramount.

questions.question: Are accelerated pathways (e.g., Fast Track, Breakthrough, PRIME, Sakigake) evaluated?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether accelerated pathway eligibility is assessed. For biologics, includes Breakthrough Therapy Designation (commonly sought for bispecifics and novel mAbs), RMAT (for advanced therapy products), and assessment of whether the biologic's MOA and clinical data support the "substantial improvement" threshold required for these designations.
questions.rationale: Bispecifics and novel mAbs have high rates of Breakthrough Therapy Designation. Strategic pursuit of these designations provides intensive FDA guidance, rolling review, and priority review — significant advantages for first-in-class biologics in competitive therapeutic areas.

questions.question: For multi-indication biologic programs, is the sequential indication filing strategy defined, including sBLA timing relative to first approval, cross-referencing of existing safety databases, incremental labeling approach, and global regulatory alignment for each indication?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether multi-indication planning includes: indication prioritisation based on regulatory feasibility, unmet need, and commercial value; sBLA submission timing relative to initial BLA approval (some indications may benefit from submission before first approval decision); cross-referencing strategy for existing safety databases across indications; incremental labeling approach (label expansion vs new labeling sections); global regulatory alignment (sequential indications may have different regulatory pathways in different regions); and immunogenicity data pooling strategy across indications.
questions.rationale: Most mAb programs pursue multiple indications sequentially. The regulatory strategy for second and subsequent indications is critical but often under-planned. sBLA timing affects patent and exclusivity strategy. Cross-referencing existing safety data reduces the burden of repeat analyses. Incremental labeling must be strategically designed. Immunogenicity data pooling across indications with different patient populations requires statistical and regulatory planning.

questions.question: Is there an experienced regulatory lead with biologic/BLA expertise assigned?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the regulatory lead has specific biologic/BLA experience. Assessment of whether the team has expertise in biologic-specific regulatory areas (comparability, immunogenicity, viral safety, combination products, biosimilar landscape).
questions.rationale: BLA regulatory strategy requires specialised expertise — the regulatory framework, guidance landscape, and common CRL causes differ fundamentally from NDA. Teams lacking biologic regulatory experience face significantly higher risk of CRLs and submission deficiencies.

questions.question: Are roles and responsibilities defined for regional regulatory activities?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether regional roles are defined.
questions.rationale: Clear roles and responsibilities are essential for coordinated global submissions.

questions.question: Is a regulatory decision-making framework in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether governance is documented.
questions.rationale: Governance frameworks are essential for timely, aligned regulatory decisions.

questions.question: Are regulatory service providers qualified and documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether service providers are qualified.
questions.rationale: Qualified providers are essential for submission quality and inspection readiness.

questions.question: Are regulatory risks identified, including biologic-specific risks (CMC comparability, immunogenicity, facility inspection, viral safety)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether risks are systematically identified. For biologics, includes CMC comparability risk from process changes, immunogenicity-related labeling risks, mandatory pre-approval facility inspection risks, viral safety documentation gaps, and for bispecifics, CRS management strategy adequacy.
questions.rationale: Biologic programmes face a distinct risk profile: 74% of BLA CRLs cite CMC deficiencies, pre-approval inspection failure is a top cause of non-approval, and immunogenicity data gaps can restrict labeling. These biologic-specific risks must be identified and mitigated prospectively.

questions.question: Is there a formal benefit-risk assessment?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a structured B-R assessment exists. For biologics, includes immunogenicity in B-R (ADA-mediated loss of efficacy vs. continued treatment benefit), CRS risk vs. anti-tumour efficacy for bispecifics, and comparison to available biologic therapies (not just standard of care).
questions.rationale: Biologic benefit-risk assessment must incorporate immunogenicity risk (a uniquely biologic consideration) and for bispecifics, the CRS/ICANS risk-benefit trade-off. FDA benefit-risk frameworks must be applied with biologic-specific risk dimensions.

questions.question: Are mitigation plans documented for key risk areas?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether mitigations are documented and tracked.
questions.rationale: Agencies and investors expect actionable risk mitigations.

questions.question: Are there known issues with platform or class-based precedents?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether platform/class issues are addressed. For biologics, includes class effects (anti-CD20 B-cell depletion → infection risk, checkpoint inhibitor irAEs, T-cell engager CRS), platform-specific regulatory precedents (bispecific format-specific safety signals), and whether these inform the programme's risk management.
questions.rationale: Biologic class effects drive substantial regulatory scrutiny. The regulatory histories of approved biologics with the same target, mechanism, or format provide critical intelligence about expected safety requirements, REMS considerations, and labeling restrictions.

questions.question: Is a draft regulatory TPP aligned with intended product labeling?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the TPP aligns with label claims. For biologics, includes immunogenicity section (USPI 6.2), dosing section (IV/SC administration instructions, weight-based vs. flat dosing), combination product labeling (if PFS/autoinjector), and biologic-specific safety sections (CRS/ICANS for bispecifics, infusion reactions).
questions.rationale: Biologic labeling contains sections with no SM equivalent: the immunogenicity subsection (USPI 6.2) reporting ADA incidence and clinical impact, detailed administration instructions for IV infusion or SC injection, and for combination products, device-specific labeling. The TPP must address these from the outset.

questions.question: Are claims in the TPP supported by available or planned data?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether TPP claims are evidence-mapped. For biologics, includes evidence planning for immunogenicity claims, comparability data supporting manufacturing consistency, and potency/efficacy data from the biological assay linked to clinical outcomes.
questions.rationale: For biologics, certain label claims require biologic-specific evidence — immunogenicity data must be characterised and reported even if ADA incidence is low, and potency claims must be linked to biological assay results, not just clinical outcomes.

questions.question: Has a target label been mapped to key efficacy and safety endpoints?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether target label is mapped to endpoints. For biologics, includes mapping of immune-mediated safety endpoints (CRS grading, infusion reactions, immunogenicity-related AEs) and biologic-specific efficacy endpoints (target cell depletion, receptor occupancy-based response).
questions.rationale: Biologic safety labeling often requires specific sections for immune-mediated adverse reactions, CRS management guidelines (for bispecifics), and infusion reaction management — these must be mapped to appropriate clinical endpoints from pivotal trials.

questions.question: Is the INN/USAN naming strategy defined per WHO INN stem conventions and FDA nonproprietary naming guidance for biologics, including proper suffix application and differentiation from potential biosimilar names?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether naming strategy includes: INN application timing (WHO INN request should be submitted early in development); compliance with WHO INN stem conventions (-mab for monoclonal antibodies, appropriate substem for target and source); FDA nonproprietary naming with distinguishing suffix per FDA guidance; coordination between INN and USAN names; bispecific-specific naming conventions (target pair naming); and differentiation strategy from future biosimilar names (biosimilars receive same core name with different suffix).
questions.rationale: Biologic naming follows specific conventions that differ from small molecule USAN naming. INN stems identify the target (-tu- for tumour, -li- for immunomodulator) and source (-u- for human, -zu- for humanised). Bispecific naming conventions are still evolving. FDA requires a distinguishing suffix for biologics. Naming errors or delays can affect trademark strategy, labeling, and market positioning. INN application should occur early in development — late application can delay BLA submission.
